content: A ball is allowed to fall from a height of $$10 \mathrm{~m}$$. If there is $$30 \%$$ loss of energy due to impact, then after one impact ball will go up to.
explanation:
When the ball falls from a height of $$10 \mathrm{~m}$$, it gains kinetic energy just before the impact which is equal to the potential energy it had at the height of $$10 \mathrm{~m}$$. The potential energy at a height $h$ is given by the formula $PE = mgh$, where $m$ is the mass of the ball, $g$ is the acceleration due to gravity (approximately $$9.8 \, \text{m/s}^2$$), and $h$ is the height from the ground.

After the impact, if there is a $$30 \%$$ loss of energy, it means that the ball retains $$70 \%$$ of its energy because the loss is $$100 \% - 70 \% = 30 \%$$. Hence, the energy with which the ball will rise after impact is $$70 \%$$ of the energy it had before the impact.

The potential energy at the height of $$10 \mathrm{~m}$$ is:

$$PE = m \cdot g \cdot 10$$

After the impact, the remaining energy is $$70 \%$$ of the initial potential energy, so the energy $E_{\text{after impact}}$ is:

$$E_{\text{after impact}} = 0.7 \cdot PE = 0.7 \cdot m \cdot g \cdot 10$$

This remaining energy will be entirely converted into potential energy again when the ball reaches its maximum height after rebounding. Let's call this maximum height $$h^\prime$$. The potential energy at height $$h^\prime$$ is:

$$PE' = m \cdot g \cdot h^\prime$$

Setting $$E_{\text{after impact}}$$ equal to $$PE'$$, we find:

$$0.7 \cdot m \cdot g \cdot 10 = m \cdot g \cdot h^\prime$$

Simplifying this, we notice that mass $$m$$ and acceleration due to gravity $$g$$ cancel out, leaving us with:

$$0.7 \cdot 10 = h^\prime$$

$$h^\prime = 7 \, \text{m}$$

Therefore, after one impact, the ball will go up to a height of $$7 \, \text{m}$$.

The correct option is Option D: 7 m.

content: A toy gun uses a spring of force constant $$k$$. When charged before being triggered in the upward direction, the spring is compressed by a distance $$x$$. If the mass of the shot is $$m$$, on being triggered it will go upto a height of
explanation:
According to law of conservation of energy, elastic potential energy stored in spring = gravitational potential energy acquired by shot.

$$\begin{aligned} \frac{1}{2} k x^2 & =m g h \\ \text{or} \quad h & =\frac{k x^2}{2 m g} \end{aligned}$$

content: A particle of mass $$m$$ is moving in a horizontal circle of radius $$r$$ under a centripetal force given by $$\left(-k / r^2\right)$$, where $$k$$ is a constant. Then

options.content: the total energy of the particle is $$(-k / 2 r)$$
the kinetic energy of the particle is $$(k / r)$$
the potential energy of the particle is $$(k / 2 r)$$
the kinetic energy of the particle is $$(-k / r)$$

explanation:
The potential energy is given by

$$\begin{aligned} U=\int F \cdot d r & =\int \frac{k}{r^2} d r=k \int r^{-2} d r \\ & =k\left[\frac{r^{-1}}{-1}\right]=-\frac{k}{r} \end{aligned}$$

The kinetic energy is given by

$$K=\frac{1}{2} m v^2=\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{k}{r}\right) \quad\left[\because \frac{m v^2}{r}=\frac{k}{r^2}\right]$$

Total energy

$$\begin{aligned} & E=U+K \\ & =-\frac{k}{r}+\frac{k}{2 r}=-\frac{k}{2 r} \end{aligned}$$

content: The power $$(P)$$ of an engine lifting a mass of $$100 \mathrm{~kg}$$ upto a height of $$10 \mathrm{~m}$$ in $$1 \mathrm{~min}$$ is
explanation:
To calculate the power required to lift a mass, we use the formula:

$$P = \frac{W}{t}$$

where:

  • $P$ is the power in watts,
  • $W$ is the work done in joules, and
  • $t$ is the time in seconds.

The work done in lifting an object is equal to the potential energy gained by the object, which can be calculated by the formula:

$$W = mgh$$

where:

  • $m$ is the mass in kilograms,
  • $g$ is the acceleration due to gravity ($9.8 \, \text{m/s}^2$), and
  • $h$ is the height in meters.

Given that:

  • $m = 100 \, \text{kg}$,
  • $h = 10 \, \text{m}$, and
  • $t = 1 \, \text{min} = 60 \, \text{seconds}$ (since time needs to be in seconds for the formula).

First, we calculate the work done:

$$W = (100 \, \text{kg}) \times (9.8 \, \text{m/s}^2) \times (10 \, \text{m}) = 9800 \, \text{J}$$

Then, we find the power:

$$P = \frac{9800 \, \text{J}}{60 \, \text{s}} = 163.3 \, \text{W}$$

Therefore, the power of the engine is $163.3 \, \text{W}$, which corresponds to Option A: P = 163.3 W.
